coredata.dc:title: Preparation and photoelectric properties of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots modified SnO2 composites
coredata.dc:creator: Lei, Yun
Wang, Yongqin
Du, Peng
Wu, Yuncui
Li, Can
Du, Beibei
Luo, Linhui
Sun, Zhengguang
Zou, Bingsuo
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots modified SnO2 composites (SnO2/N-GQDs) were prepared by a solvothermal method, where N-GQDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal process with citric acid and urea as sources. The lattice stripes of 0.33 nm, 0.26 nm and 0.24 nm were ascribed to (110) and (101) crystal planes of SnO2 and (1120) crystal plane of N-GQDs, respectively. The photoelectric properties of SnO2/N-GQDs composites with different N-GQDs contents were characterized using I-t, LSV, EIS, and Mott-Schottky. Among them, SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites reached the smallest electrochemical impedance and achieved the optimal transient photocurrent value of 1.880 × 10−4 A/cm2. Compared with pure SnO2, SnO2/N-GQDs-2 presented a 5-fold increase in the photocurrent density. LSV showed that the incorporation of N-GQDs could enhance the photocurrent intensity of SnO2. The Mott-Schottky slope of SnO2/N-GQDs-2 in light was smaller than that of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs-2 in dark, indicating that more carriers were generated under UV illumination and transferred with the doping of N-GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
SnO2/N-GQDs composites
Photoelectric properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: AFM, Raman, PL and UV–visible analysis of N-GQDs
XRD analysis of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
FT-IR analysis of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
UV–visible analysis of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
I-t and LSV analysis of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
EIS of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
Mott-Schottky analysis of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM image and height profile of N-GQDs.
UV–visible spectrum of N-GQDs (a), PL spectra of N-GQDs (b).
XRD patterns of pure SnO2 (a) and SnO2/N-GQDs composites (b).
TEM image (a) and HRTEM image of SnO2/N-GQDs composites (b).
Full scan XPS spectrum of the SnO2/N-GQDs composites (a), high-resolution Sn 3d (b), O 1s (c), C 1s (d), and N 1s (e).
FTIR spectra of pure SnO2 (a), SnO2/N-GQDs-1 (b), SnO2/N-GQDs-2 (c), SnO2/N-GQDs-3 (d), SnO2/N-GQDs-4 (e).
UV–visible spectra (a) and Tauc's plots (b) of pure SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites.
Transient photocurrent responses of pure SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites (a), the stability test of SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites (b).
Linear sweep voltammetry curves of pure SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites.
EIS Nyquist plots (a) and fitting curves (b) of pure SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs.
Mott-Schottky plots of pure SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites under dark conditions (a), Mott-Schottky plots of SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites under light and dark conditions (b).

head.ce:title: Preparation and photoelectric properties of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots modified SnO2 composites

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of resources and environmental engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots modified SnO2 composites (SnO2/N-GQDs) were prepared by a solvothermal method, where N-GQDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal process with citric acid and urea as sources. The lattice stripes of 0.33 nm, 0.26 nm and 0.24 nm were ascribed to (110) and (101) crystal planes of SnO2 and (1120) crystal plane of N-GQDs, respectively. The photoelectric properties of SnO2/N-GQDs composites with different N-GQDs contents were characterized using I-t, LSV, EIS, and Mott-Schottky. Among them, SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites reached the smallest electrochemical impedance and achieved the optimal transient photocurrent value of 1.880 × 10−4 A/cm2. Compared with pure SnO2, SnO2/N-GQDs-2 presented a 5-fold increase in the photocurrent density. LSV showed that the incorporation of N-GQDs could enhance the photocurrent intensity of SnO2. The Mott-Schottky slope of SnO2/N-GQDs-2 in light was smaller than that of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs-2 in dark, indicating that more carriers were generated under UV illumination and transferred with the doping of N-GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Tin dioxide (SnO2) is a characteristic wide-band gap n-type semiconductor (Eg = 3.6 eV at 27 °C) [1], which is extensively applied in fuel cells [2], lithium-ion batteries [3], gas sensors [4] and supercapacitors [5]. As nanoscale graphene, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), owing to their quantum confinement and edge effects [6,7], have a wide-range of applications such as sensors [8], energy sources [9], photodetectors [10,11], optoelectronic devices [12], drug delivery [13,14], photocatalyst [15], etc. The introduction of heteroatoms into the carbon lattice of GQDs, which can effectively adjust the local optical, chemical and electronic performances of GQDs [16,17]. Among them, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) can improve photoelectrochemical performance and photoactivity [18–21]. Studies have shown that the doping of GQDs into semiconductors can effectively improve material performance. Zhang et al. [22] achieved a high performance acetone gas sensor base on ZnO/S, N-GQDs/PANI thin film. Guo et al. [23] synthesized P-GQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst with efficient photocatalytic activity towards MO degradation under UV light irradiation. Lv et al. [24] successfully employed a simple sulfidation-oxidation method to synthesize N-GQDs modified SnO2 composites with excellent detection ability for low concentration NO2. Chen et al. [25] synthesized N-GQDs/SnO2 nano composites with high sensitivity to HCHO. Hsu et al. [26] prepared SnO2/PANI/N-GQD nanocomposite with a good linear response to dopamine. Although there were some studies on gas-sensing performances of N-GQDs/SnO2 composites, literature integrating photoelectron separation and transfer was scarcely reported, moreover, the influence of N-GQDs content on the photoelectric properties of SnO2 was scarcely studied.

In this paper, SnO2/N-GQDs composites were synthesized through a solvothermal process. With the help of various characterization methods, the morphology, structure and chemical composition of the prepared composites were analyzed, and their photoelectric performances were studied. The introduction of N-GQDs into SnO2 semiconductor materials could effectively reduce the electron-hole recombination efficiency, expedite the transition of photoinduced electrons, and improve the performance of the composites.

All chemicals are analytical grade for direct use. Citric acid (CA), urea (CO(NH2)2), tin chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O), anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH), hydrochloric acid (HCl 0.1 mol/L), Nafion solution (5.47%) and dialysis bag (3500 Da, molecular weight cut off).

0.21 g of CA and 0.6 g of urea were dispersed in 5 mL of deionized water, and the solution was sonicated and poured into an autoclave to heat at 180 °C for 4 h. The supernatant of the product was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μm disposable needle filter and dialyzed for 48 h [27]. The concentration of the prepared N-GQDs was 0.758 mg/mL.
⁃
2.2.2 Preparation of SnO2 nanoparticles

At first, 1g of SnCl4·5H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of HCl (0.1 mol/L), and then 150 mg of urea was slowly dispersed in the mixture. After the mixture was autoclaved at 200 °C for 10 h, the solvothermal products were then centrifuged several times with ethanol and deionized water. Finally, SnO2 nanoparticles were obtained by desiccation for 12 h at 60 °C.
⁃
2.2.3 Preparation of SnO2/N-GQDs nanoparticles

1g of SnCl4 · 5H2O was dissolved into 20 mL of HCl (0.1 mol/L). Different amounts of N-GQDs (0.5 mL, 1 mL, 1.5 mL, 2 mL) were added separately and sonicated for 30 min. Then, 150 mg urea was slowly transferred into the mixture until it was completely dissolved, and the mixture was autoclaved at 200 °C for 10 h [28]. The solvothermal products were then centrifuged with deionized water and ethanol repeatedly, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. After the product was ground and annealed in nitrogen atmosphere at 360 °C for 1h, the composites with different content were prepared, noted as SnO2/N-GQDs-1, SnO2/N-GQDs-2, SnO2/N-GQDs-3 and SnO2/N-GQDs-4, respectively.

The structural information was characterized by a D/MAX-RB X-ray diffractometer. The morphology of the samples was observed by atomic force microscopy and transmission electron microscopy (AFM, NT-MDT Prima and TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, A Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) was used for elemental analysis. The functional groups in the samples were analyzed by Fourier transform Infrared (FTIR, Nicolet, IS-10). The optical properties of the samples were researched by UV–visible spectra (UV-5500 spectrophotometer) and photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL, Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer). The Raman spectra of samples were performed on a RENISHAW Raman microscope.

The electrochemical experiments were recorded by an electrochemical workstation (CHI-650E). The transient photocurrent measurements, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky measurements were performed using a three-electrode system. As shown in Scheme 1 , as FTO glass coated with samples was served as the working electrode, a standard calomel electrode and a platinum foil were applied as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The electrolyte was 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 solution. The working electrodes were prepared as follows: 50 mg of SnO2 or SnO2/N-GQDs composites was evenly coated onto a FTO conductive glass slice as the working electrode and the active area was 1.0 cm2. The amperometric i-t curve was measured at 0.5 V via several repeated on/off illumination cycles. The scanning rate of LSV was 0.01 V S−1. EIS was measured from 106 Hz to 10−2 Hz. Mott-Schottky measurements were performed at an amplitude of 10 mV and a fixed frequency of 2000 Hz. The three-electrode system was placed in darkness for dark field measurements, and the working electrode was continuously irradiated by a point light source (365 nm) for bright field measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AFM, Raman, PL and UV–visible analysis of N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 displayed the AFM image and height profile of the prepared N-GQDs. The distribution of N-GQDs was relatively uniform with an average height of about 3 nm. These results revealed that the prepared N-GQDs had the average height of 6 layers graphene structure [29].

The Raman spectral data of N-GQDs were shown in Fig. 2 . Two prominent peaks at 1347 cm−1 (D-band) and 1589 cm−1 (G-band) were associated with sp3 defects and the planar vibrations of sp2 carbon, respectively. The results showed an intensity ratio (ID/IG) of 0.97, which was nearly to that of RGO [30], indicating that the prepared N-GQDs was dominated by the formation RGO.

UV–visible and PL spectra of N-GQDs optical properties were shown in Fig. 3 a and b, respectively. In the ultraviolet region, the absorption peak at 239 nm corresponded to the π→π* transition (CC) [31]. The peak at 331 nm was related to the n→π∗ electron transition (CO) [32,33]. In Fig. 3a, when the excitation wavelength increased from 280 to 480, the PL intensity of N-GQDs first increased and then gradually weakened, and the PL intensity reached the maximum at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XRD analysis of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: XRD was used to analyze and characterize the crystal structure of pure SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites. As shown in Fig. 4 , there were peaks at 26.58°, 33.82°, 37.9° and 51.82° corresponding to the (110), (101), (200) and (211) SnO2 crystal planes. These diffraction peaks corresponded to tetragonal rutile SnO2 (JCPDS No.41-1445) [34]. Due to the small amount of N-GQDs, no correlation peak appeared [35]. In addition, no other impurity phase was observed in XRD patterns, which indicated the high purity of the samples. Comparing the XRD patterns of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs, it was found that the relevant peak positions of SnO2 were almost unchanged after the addition of N-GQDs, indicating that the addition of N-GQDs had no effect on the crystal structure of SnO2.

Fig. 5 a and b showed TEM and HETEM images of SnO2/N-GQDs, respectively. The magnitude of nanoparticles was approximately 10 nm. The HRTEM image of SnO2/N-GQDs presented the spacing of 0.26 nm, 0.33 nm and 0.24 nm corresponding to the (101) and (110) planes of SnO2, and (1120) plane of N-GQDs, respectively.

The elemental and chemical components of the SnO2/N-GQDs were studied using XPS. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the composites consisted of Sn, O, C and N elements. The Sn 3d spectrum of SnO2/N-GQDs (Fig. 6b) contained two peaks at 495.3 and 486.88 eV indexed to Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2, respectively. Compared with the pure SnO2 prepared by similar methods previously [36], the XPS peaks position of Sn in the composites was shifted. Because of the electron transfer from SnO2 to N-GQDs, the peak of Sn shifted to the higher binding energy [37]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 6c) was resolved into three components including 530.75 eV of O–C, 531.86 eV of OC, and 532.79 eV of O–H, respectively. The spectrum of the C 1s region was decomposed into three peaks, suggesting the presence of carbon in three separate chemical environments (Fig. 6d). Three peaks containing 284.73 eV, 286.21 eV and 288.89 eV corresponded to sp2 hybridized C–C/CC and sp3 hybridized C–O/CO bonds, respectively. Fig. 6e showed the N 1s peaks of SnO2/N-GQDs composites, and the three fitted peaks at about 400.87eV, 400.05eV, 399.25eV, could be attributed to graphitic N, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N atoms [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR analysis of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 exhibited the FTIR spectra of the samples. All samples presented a wide absorption peak at 3418 cm−1 and a weak absorption peak at 1400 cm−1 due to O–H vibration from H2O in air [38]. In Fig. 7b–e, the wavenumbers around 616 cm−1 and 503 cm−1 were related to Sn–O–Sn and Sn–O stretching vibration, which was consistent with the characteristic stretching vibration of pure SnO2 (Fig. 7a). The wavenumber around 1622 cm−1 was due to O–H bending vibration [39]. The wavenumbers around 1630 cm−1 and 1050 cm−1 were indexed to CO and C–O–C stretching vibration [39,40], which were consistent with the XPS analysis and became more pronounced as the amount of N-GQDs increased. The wavenumbers around 1457 cm−1 (Fig. 7d and e) were ascribed to C–N stretching vibration of N-GQDs [29], which were not observed in Fig. 7b (SnO2/N-GQDs-1) and 7c (SnO2/N-GQDs-2) due to the low content of N-GQDs in the composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible analysis of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 a showed the UV–visible spectra of the pure SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites. In pure SnO2, two absorption peaks appeared at 220 nm and 282 nm, which corresponded to the characteristic absorption peaks of SnO2. Compared with the peak of N-GQDs at 331 nm (Fig. 3a), the strength of the composites at this position was significantly weakened. This phenomenon was caused by the fact that most of the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of N-GQDs were removed during the solvothermal preparation of SnO2/N-GQDs composites. Compared with pure SnO2, the absorption peak position of the composites almost did not change. The corresponding band gap (Eg) was estimated in kubelka-Munk equation and the result was shown in Fig. 8b. Because the amount of N-GQDs was relatively low, the band gap of the material was slightly affected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: I-t and LSV analysis of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 a showed the amperometric i-t curves of the pure SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs. The responses increased sharply when UV light was on, and gradually dropped when the light was turned off. Moreover, the N-GQDs content on the photocurrent responses of SnO2/N-GQDs was investigated. The photocurrent value of the composites increased as the N-GQDs content increased, and reached a maximum at 1.880 × 10−4 A/cm2 of SnO2/N-GQDs-2, while the instantaneous photocurrent response of the composites gradually declined as the N-GQDs further increased. As the light was on, the photocurrent reached the maximum following a continuous decrease. Such photocurrent decay indicated that charge recombination processes were occurring. During the decay process, the holes accumulated at the SnO2 surface competitively recombine with electrons from the CB of SnO2. The slow response of the photocurrent decaying to zero after turning off the irradiation was controlled by charge carriers released from the traps [41]. It suggested that N-GQDs could separate and store the photogenerated electrons, consequently reducing the recombination rate of the photogenerated electrons and holes and enhancing the production of photocurrent. In comparison with pure SnO2, SnO2/N-GQDs-2 exhibited up to a 5-fold increase in the instantaneous photocurrent response [42]. In order to demonstrate the stability of SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites, the photocurrent curve as a function of time was performed and presented in Fig. 9b. The SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites maintained a relatively stable light response. Fig. 10 showed the LSV curves of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites. From the LSV curves, it could be seen that the photocurrent intensity increased significantly after doping with N-GQDs, which corresponded to the analytical results of photocurrent responses [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of further understanding the electrochemical properties of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites, the interfacial electron transfer of the composites was studied by electrochemical impedance. EIS Nyquist plots and the fitting curves of pure SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs was shown in Fig. 11 a and b, respectively. The equivalent circuit chart of the system was presented in the inset of Fig. 11b. CPE indicated the double-layer capacitance and Rs showed electrolyte resistance, respectively. The linear part of the low frequency was caused by the diffusion process impedance called Warburg impedance (Rw). Rct was the resistance of the deposited layer and represented, to some extent, the impedance of the interface contact. It could be analyzed by using the semi-circular arc in the high frequency region. Large semi-circular arc meant high interfacial resistance and high impedance during electron transport. By comparing the curves in Fig. 11b, it could be found that the interfacial impedance of the composites first decreased and then increased with the increase of N-GQDs content. This trend was ascribed to the reason that appropriate amount of N-GQDs were beneficial to integrate with SnO2 and promote the separation of photocarriers. However, when the doping amount exceeded a certain ratio, N-GQDs tended to aggregate due to their small size and high surface energy, which hindered the separation and transmission of photocarriers. As shown in Fig. 11b, the resistance in the process of interfacial electron conduction of SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites was smaller than that of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs with other N-GQDs contents. And the Rct value of pure SnO2 was 44.00 Ω, while that of SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites decreased to 33.34Ω. Therefore, At SnO2/N-GQDs-2, it had the smallest electrochemical impedance, which was consistent with the analysis results of instantaneous photocurrent response [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mott-Schottky analysis of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs composites
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 12 showed the Mott-Schottky plots of SnO2 and SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites for determining the carrier density (ND) of the semiconductors. The samples all exhibited a positive Mott-Schottky curve slope, which was consistent with the typical shape of n-type semiconductors. According to the Mott-Schottky equation:
C
− 2
= 2 / ε
ε 0
e
N D
( V −
V
F B
−
k B
T
e
− 1
)
, ND was related to the slope of the curve. By estimating the slopes of the linear part of the Mott-Schottky curves, it was found that the Mott-Schottky slope of the SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites was smaller than that of SnO2 (Fig. 12a). This was ascribed to the doping of N-GQDs into SnO2 as charge transporters, which promoted the separation of photogenerated carriers and reduced the electron-hole recombination rate. Furthermore, the Mott-Schottky slope of the SnO2/N-GQDs-2 composites became smaller under UV irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 12b). This phenomenon was due to the fact that more electrons were generated under UV irradiation and the introduction of N-GQDs enhanced the electron transfer, which further raised the photoelectric conversion efficiency [46,47]. The transient photocurrent response, linear sweep voltammetry curves and EIS Nyquist analysis results were consistent.

The photoelectric mechanism of SnO2/N-GQDs composites was shown in Fig. 13 . In the light field, the working electrode (Scheme 1) was irradiated by UV light (365 nm), electrons were stimulated to the conduction band (CB) from the valence band (VB) of SnO2, while holes were left in the VB. Without the doping of N-GQDs, photogenerated electrons were easily recombined with holes, which led to low transfer efficiency of electrons and poor photoelectric conversion efficiency of the SnO2. In SnO2/N-GQDs composites, however, N-GQDs acted as charge transfer carriers, which could rapidly transfer photoelectrons to the CB, prevent electron-hole recombination, improve the transfer efficiency of electrons, and effectively enhance the photoelectric properties of SnO2 [48].

In summary, SnO2/N-GQDs composites had been successfully prepared by the solvothermal process. The average height of N-GQDs was 3 nm with about 6 graphene sheets. TEM results showed that the interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm corresponded to the (1120) crystal face of N-GQDs, and the interplanar spacing of 0.33 nm and 0.26 nm corresponded to the (110) and (101) crystal faces of SnO2, respectively. Combined with the analysis results of I-t and EIS Nyquist plots, SnO2/N-GQDs-2 presented the lowest electrochemical impedance, and the optimal transient photocurrent of 1.880 × 10−4 A/cm2. LSV analysis also indicated that there was an obvious increase in photocurrent intensity after doping with N-GQDs. Mott-Schottky analysis suggested that our prepared SnO2/N-GQDs composites had more carriers under UV irradiation. In conclusion, the doping of N-GQDs could effectively promote the efficiency of electron transition, reducing electron-hole recombination efficiency, which further enhanced the optoelectronic conversion efficiency.

Yun Lei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – Review & Editing, Validation. Yongqin Wang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – Original Draft. Peng Du: Investigation. Yuncui Wu, Can Li, Beibei Du, Linhui Luo: Visualization. Zhengguang Sun: review & editing. Bingsuo Zou: Revision.
Zhengguang Sun: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51204129) and open foundation of Guangxi Key Laboratory of Processing for Non-ferrous Metals and Featured Materials, Guangxi University (Grant No. 2021GXYSOF09).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoelectric properties of SnO2 decorated by graphene quantum dots
Mater. Sci. Semicond. Process.

Energy. 39 (2014) 10724-10730. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijhydene.2014.05.008.
